metadata.title: Claude, Your Quizmaster.
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. Do not retint.
metadata.greeting_note: HAI persona, shortest-form rule. 'Hej' = Scandinavian casual, one word.
metadata.thesis: The best use of an answer machine is making it ask the questions.
metadata.note: ILLUSTRATE LAW reel: UI at B00 (cold open with RESULT lines — the quizmaster capabilities), B06 (verdict artifact), B07 (handoff), B08 (outro). Inner beats B01–B05 each use a distinct custom Remotion scene on cream stage. PREDICT at B02 (predict-before-reveal as a study move doubles as the episode's own PREDICT moment — narration cues the commit before explaining retrieval practice). COLD OPEN, SPARK-LINE, HANDOFF, OUTRO laws in force. Mid-career cut at B05. House red line: teaches active study with AI as quizmaster, never teaches submitting AI output as own work.

then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: The predict-before-reveal move caught me off guard — being asked to commit to what retrieval practice is before the definition appeared, because the attempt itself drives encoding. I want to build this into my studying. Help me: (1) turn a chapter I'm learning into a set of predict-first prompts where I answer before any reveal; (2) explain why a wrong-but-committed guess still strengthens memory more than passively reading the answer; (3) layer the spaced schedule on top so each predict-first attempt lands just as the material is fading.

props.command: The predict-before-reveal move caught me off guard — being asked to commit to what retrieval practice is before the definition appeared, because the attempt itself drives encoding. I want to build this into my studying. Help me: (1) turn a chapter I'm learning into a set of predict-first prompts where I answer before any reveal; (2) explain why a wrong-but-committed guess still strengthens memory more than passively reading the answer; (3) layer the spaced schedule on top so each predict-first attempt lands just as the material is fading.
output: spaced schedule — one week, one month out

beats.narration_text: Why quizzing works. When you read a chapter, the information goes in. When you test yourself, you have to pull it back out. That act of pulling — retrieval — is what strengthens the memory trace. The testing effect: testing yourself is not a check on whether you learned something. It is a significant part of how you learn it. Rereading feels productive because you recognize the words. Testing reveals what you actually own.

props.heading: why quizzing works
props.body: Retrieval — pulling the answer out yourself — strengthens the memory trace. The testing effect: testing yourself is not a check on whether you learned something. It is a significant part of how you learn it. Rereading feels productive because you recognize the words. Testing reveals what you actually own.

beats.narration_text: Try it now — this is the move itself. Before I explain how to use the predict-before-reveal technique: commit to what retrieval practice is. Not after you've seen this card. Right now, in your head: what is retrieval practice? The commitment is what drives encoding. Your answer doesn't have to be right — the attempt is the mechanism. Now here's the definition.

props.heading: how to use it
props.body: Commit before you see the answer — not after. The commitment is what drives encoding. When you predict first, your brain is in retrieval mode. The answer that follows either confirms or corrects. Both outcomes strengthen the trace. After-the-fact recognition is not the same mechanism.

beats.narration_text: Space the repetitions. One quiz today. Another tomorrow. A third next week. A fourth a month out. Memory decays — that's not a flaw, it's the system. Spaced practice catches the information just as it's fading, re-consolidates it, and extends the retention window each time. The schedule isn't hard to build. Paste the topic into Claude and ask for a spaced quiz schedule. It will tell you exactly when to come back.

The explain-it-back move. You tell Claude the concept — not Claude telling you. You explain what photosynthesis is, or what Keynesian economics means, or what the third amendment covers. Claude reads your explanation and tells you what's right, what's incomplete, and what you missed. The gaps surface when you hesitate or get something wrong. That hesitation is information. It tells you exactly what to study next.

props.heading: the explain-it-back move
props.body: You explain the concept to Claude; gaps surface when you get it wrong or hesitate. Tell Claude what you think you know. It reads your explanation and shows you what's right, incomplete, or missing. The hesitation is the signal — it tells you exactly what to study next.

beats.narration_text: Same moves, your field. If you're mid-career — new regulation, new platform, new product line — these techniques transfer exactly. Quiz yourself on the new compliance framework. Explain the new process back to Claude and find your gaps. Space your reviews across the first month. Claude doesn't know your industry's edge. You bring that. The tool handles the repetition mechanics. You bring the judgment about what matters.

props.heading: for mid-career learners
props.body: Same techniques apply to workplace material — quiz yourself on the new process, the new regulatory change, the new product. Claude doesn't know your industry's edge; you bring that. The tool handles repetition mechanics. You bring the judgment about what matters in your context.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The study stack in full. Quiz not answer — you do the retrieval, Claude is the stimulus. Commit before the reveal — the commitment is the mechanism, not the confirmation. Space the reviews — today, tomorrow, next week, next month. Explain it back — your explanation surfaces the gaps faster than rereading ever would. Four moves. One principle: the struggle is the learning.

artifactLines: Quiz not answer: you do the retrieval, Claude is the question generator. Testing beats rereading — the testing effect is real.
Commit before the reveal: predict first, then see the answer. Commitment drives encoding. After-the-fact recognition is not the same.
Space the reviews: today · +1 day · +1 week · +1 month. Memory decays — spacing catches it at the fade.
Explain it back: tell Claude what you know; it shows you the gaps. Hesitation is information.

beats.narration_text: Your move. Paste this in with your chapter or topic. Claude will quiz you — one question at a time — and give you a spaced schedule for when to come back. Replace the bracket with your actual material.

props.command: Quiz me on [topic or chapter]. Ask one question at a time. Wait for my answer. Tell me if I'm right and why. After 5 questions, give me a spaced review schedule: when should I quiz again to lock this in?
